prompts.prompt: Our architecture review board rejected using AWS DynamoDB because it creates vendor lock-in. We need to use PostgreSQL on EC2 instead. Is this the right call?
prompts.reason: Classic over-insuring against lock-in — needs ROL calculation and U-curve analysis

prompts.prompt: We're building a Kubernetes abstraction layer so our applications can run on EKS, GKE, or AKS without modification. Is this a good way to avoid cloud lock-in?
prompts.reason: Esperanto Effect risk — abstraction layer creates new lock-in while blocking native capabilities

prompts.prompt: AWS is offering us enterprise support, dedicated TAMs, and favorable pricing in exchange for making AWS our primary platform. Should we accept?
prompts.reason: ROL evaluation — conscious lock-in with tangible benefits; needs benefit vs. switching cost analysis

prompts.prompt: How do I make the case to my engineering team that avoiding all cloud-specific services is actually costing us more than any future migration would?
prompts.reason: Explaining the U-curve / over-insurance argument — total cost vs. switching cost minimization

prompts.prompt: Our team insists on using only open-source, cloud-agnostic tools so we can switch providers any time. The self-management overhead is significant. How do I evaluate this trade-off?
prompts.reason: Underutilization cost of portability investment — needs explicit U-curve analysis

prompts.prompt: We have lock-in to our current CI/CD platform, our cloud provider, and our container orchestration system. Which type of lock-in matters most and how do we prioritize?
prompts.reason: Eight-dimension taxonomy — different lock-in types have different probability and switching cost profiles

prompts.prompt: Our architecture principle says 'avoid cloud vendor lock-in.' We're trying to use it to make decisions but it doesn't help us decide anything — every option seems to violate it. What's wrong?
prompts.reason: Principle failure — 'avoid lock-in' without a threshold or cost model is not an actionable principle; Gregor's First Law context

prompts.prompt: We spent $2M building a multi-cloud abstraction layer. Now we're told we can't use managed ML services because they're not in the abstraction. Was this worth it?
prompts.reason: Post-hoc ROL analysis — underutilization cost of Esperanto layer vs. switching cost reduction achieved

prompts.prompt: How do I configure AWS IAM roles for a Lambda function?
prompts.reason: Operational configuration task — no lock-in trade-off decision involved

prompts.prompt: What is the SLA for Google Cloud SQL?
prompts.reason: Vendor specification lookup — not a lock-in cost analysis

prompts.prompt: How does Kubernetes rolling deployment work?
prompts.reason: Technical concept explanation — no lock-in optimization decision

prompts.prompt: We're migrating from on-premises to AWS. What order should we migrate our applications?
prompts.reason: Migration sequencing question — related to hybrid cloud strategy, not lock-in cost optimization

prompts.prompt: How do we negotiate better pricing with our cloud provider?
prompts.reason: Commercial negotiation — tangentially related to ROL but the core question is procurement, not architectural lock-in optimization

prompts.prompt: We're a government agency required by regulation to use multiple cloud providers and to be able to switch providers within 90 days. How do we approach this?
prompts.reason: Regulatory mandate for portability changes the expected switching probability to near-1.0 — the framework applies differently when the switch is mandated, not merely possible; the portability investment is no longer optional

prompts.prompt: We accepted significant AWS lock-in three years ago based on ROL reasoning. Now AWS has raised prices 40%. Should we have built in more portability?
prompts.reason: Retroactive ROL evaluation — the framework's probability estimates were proven wrong; this is the legitimate tail-risk scenario the model understates; boundary between 'model was wrong' and 'model was right and we had bad luck'

prompts.prompt: We want to use Cloud Spanner for its globally-distributed transaction capability. No other product does this. Is using it a lock-in risk we should worry about?
prompts.reason: Unique capability with high switching cost — strong ROL case, but also represents a genuine architecture lock-in if the product is discontinued or pricing changes radically; needs both ROL analysis and risk assessment
